snippets.title: scikit-learn RandomForestClassifier with OOB score
snippets.code:
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score

X, y = load_breast_cancer(return_X_y=True)

rf = RandomForestClassifier(
    n_estimators=200,
    max_features="sqrt",
    bootstrap=True,
    oob_score=True,
    random_state=0,
)
rf.fit(X, y)
print("OOB accuracy:", round(rf.oob_score_, 3))

cv = cross_val_score(rf, X, y, cv=5, scoring="accuracy")
print("CV accuracy mean:", round(cv.mean(), 3))
snippets.explanation: Default forest uses bootstrap=True and max_features='sqrt'. oob_score gives a built-in holdout estimate per tree.

snippets.title: Compare single tree vs random forest variance on CV folds
snippets.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
X = rng.normal(size=(400, 8))
y = (X[:, 0] + X[:, 1] > 0).astype(int)

for name, model in [
    ("single tree", DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=None)),
    ("random forest", RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=0)),
]:
    scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=5, scoring="accuracy")
    print(f"{name}: mean={scores.mean():.3f}, std={scores.std():.3f}")
snippets.explanation: Tighter cross-fold score spread for the forest illustrates variance reduction from bagging and feature randomness.

snippets.title: Feature importance from mean decrease in impurity
snippets.code:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.datasets import fetch_california_housing

X, y = fetch_california_housing(return_X_y=True, as_frame=True)

rf = RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=100, max_features=0.33, random_state=0)
rf.fit(X, y)

imp = pd.Series(rf.feature_importances_, index=X.columns).sort_values(ascending=False)
print(imp.head())
snippets.explanation: Forests expose aggregate feature importance across trees—useful for exploration, not causal inference.
